chain_of_thought: Native stack trace shows only an unknown crash site ("??") above Java_io_realm_internal_TableQuery_nativeRawDescriptor in librealm-jni.so; actual failing instruction and allocator/sanitizer frames are missing, so I cannot identify a specific memory operation or bug location.

I attempted to use Ghidra MCP (search_functions_by_name) to decompile Java_io_realm_internal_TableQuery_nativeRawDescriptor in the opened librealm-jni.so, but the tool backend is unreachable (connection refused), so no native code or call graph inside the .so can be inspected.
Without decompilation, I cannot perform the mandated backward data-flow from any memcpy/memmove-like operations, nor can I see how the descriptor string is parsed or validated in native code.

On the Java side, the callgraph shows that nativeRawDescriptor is called via TableQuery.rawDescriptor/sort/distinct from RealmQuery.sort/distinct, with ultimate inputs being column/field names used for sorting or distinct queries, which are simple Java strings typically constrained to model field names in Realm; these are not obviously fully attacker-controlled beyond what app developers specify in code.

The fuzz harness likely invokes nativeRawDescriptor with arbitrary or malformed values (e.g., invalid query pointer J, random descriptor strings, or out-of-lifetime Realm objects), conditions that production Java code would not create because TableQuery instances and descriptors are constructed by the Realm SDK itself, not by arbitrary user input.

Realm is a mature persistence library; its JNI entry points typically perform internal assertions and argument checks. A crash under a greybox fuzzer that passes random jlong handles or strings does not on its own demonstrate an exploitable vulnerability if such arguments cannot be arbitrarily set from realistic app or remote inputs.

Because I lack evidence of specific unsafe operations (no visible memcpy/array access), no information about the termination reason (signal 11 vs abort/assert), and no way to tie the crash to attacker-controllable inputs (beyond unrealistic fuzz-only handles), I cannot confidently label this as a genuine vulnerability.

Given the constraints, the most defensible classification is that this is likely a harness- or environment-induced crash (e.g., corrupt/invalid TableQuery handle, misuse of Realm lifecycle) rather than a demonstrable, remotely triggerable memory-safety bug; classification as vulnerable would be speculative.

However, since I cannot rule out a native bug inside nativeRawDescriptor (e.g., unvalidated string-based query descriptor leading to oob access), I set a moderate but sub-0.5 confidence for non-vulnerability and recommend further manual Ghidra analysis when the tooling is available.
reasons: Crash site and termination reason are unknown; no allocator/sanitizer evidence or specific unsafe native operation visible.
Could not decompile librealm-jni.so due to MCP backend unavailability, so no proof of memory corruption or missing bounds checks in nativeRawDescriptor.
Java callgraph suggests descriptor strings and query objects are framework-constructed, not directly attacker-controlled; fuzz harness likely passes unrealistic jlong handles.
recommendations: Re-run analysis with functioning Ghidra MCP to decompile Java_io_realm_internal_TableQuery_nativeRawDescriptor and inspect how it interprets descriptor strings and jlong handles.
Instrument runtime with AddressSanitizer/UBSan and capture a full native stack (with symbols) and signal/abort details to see whether the failure is due to memory corruption or internal assertion.

Construct targeted Java-level tests that call TableQuery.rawDescriptor/sort/distinct with extreme-but-valid descriptors and observe whether any crashes are reachable without violating Realm’s documented usage.
assumptions: TableQuery and descriptor arguments in production are created via Realm APIs and not filled with arbitrary untrusted data.
The fuzz harness may be passing invalid/expired native handles or violating Realm’s expected lifecycles.
No other evidence of Realm library vulnerabilities is provided beyond this single crash entry.
limitations: Ghidra MCP backend is unreachable, preventing any decompilation of native functions in librealm-jni.so.
No detailed crash reason (signal, abort message, register state) or top-of-stack native frame is available.
Cannot inspect Java sources for RealmQuery/TableQuery and app code to verify how descriptor strings are formed and whether any untrusted input can influence them.
